Bastani answers: Do they understand real semiconductor execution? Karin Raj answers: Do they understand telecom, RF, and customer qualification? Heine answers: Can the financial story become cleaner? Helena answers: Can the board think strategically about M&A, capital markets, and which future deserves capital? The next one is Joakim Nideborn. And I think his question is slightly different: Can the $SIVE story move from pipeline and promise to numbers serious investors can underwrite? Joakim was added to the $SIVE board at the latest AGM. But the more important detail is that he was elected Vice Chairman. Sivers also said he would have primary board oversight for customers, employees, and investors in the Nordic region. That is specific. So I do not read this as just “new board member.” I read it as Sivers adding someone for the translation layer: technology story -> investor language pipeline -> revenue quality assets -> organization acquisitions -> integration growth plan -> cash flow That is Joakim’s lane. He is CFO of $HMS.ST. CFO since 2017. Deputy CEO since 2022. Before that, CFO at Beijer Electronics Group. Before that, management and strategy consulting at Axholmen and Celerant. So the pattern is pretty consistent: industrial technology, finance, operations, strategy, public-company communication. At $HMS.ST, Joakim has been inside a listed Swedish industrial technology company with real products, global customers, hardware/software, acquisitions, production, working capital, margins, and public-market reporting. From HMS’ 2025 annual report: Net sales: 2021: SEK 1.97B 2025: SEK 3.58B Adjusted EBIT: SEK 466M -> SEK 911M Operating cash flow: SEK 508M -> SEK 877M 2025 adjusted operating margin: ~25.5% So he has operated inside a company where industrial technology is translated into margins, cash flow, and public-market discipline. For $SIVE this is highly relevant. The second part is integration. HMS made its two largest acquisitions in 2024: Red Lion Controls in the US. PEAK-System in Germany. In 2025, HMS worked on integrating them into the organization. Red Lion and N-Tron became product brands under HMS. PEAK became part of the New Industries division. HMS also reduced its debt ratio from 3.4x EBITDA at the beginning of the year to 2.2x by year-end, helped by strong cash flow. That is not a small detail. Because $SIVE is also partly an integration story. MixComm brought US mmWave IP. Sivers Photonics / CST brought the Glasgow photonics fab. Wireless depends on manufacturing partners. Photonics needs capacity, qualification, and customer ramp discipline. So the question is not just whether the assets are good. The question is whether they become one coherent company. That is where Joakim’s background becomes interesting. Acquisitions are easy to announce. Integration is where the truth shows up. The third part is Nordic credibility. This may be the most overlooked part of his appointment. Sivers is talking about a potential US listing, PCAOB-related reporting work, and a more international investor base. But it cannot skip the Nordic trust layer. There is a running joke on X that Sweden is unusually good at doubting its own speculative small caps. And honestly, with $SIVE, you can see why that matters. The story has attracted global attention, but the home market still wants the boring proof: clean reporting, clear disclosure, capital discipline, governance, incentives, and whether the pipeline is being translated into numbers. That is why Joakim’s Nordic oversight role matters. Sivers specifically said he would have primary board oversight for customers, employees, and investors in the Nordic region. That is not a throwaway line. He has been CFO of a respected listed Swedish industrial-tech company. So he likely understands the language Nordic investors respond to: less hype, more numbers, more structure, more disclosure, more proof. If $SIVE wants to win trust at home while preparing for a broader international investor base, he is one of the keys."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2067530466337419464"}}, "rest_id": "2068979173910040972", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "24889", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2071713043990368488", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "14266", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
At @OpticaWorldwide  Quantum  Summit in Glasgow, one challenge surfaced repeatedly across discussions  on quantum computing, networking, and photonics: the industry needs  higher accuracy and lower loss.

As light moves from being a communication medium to becoming part of the computational fabric, the performance requirements for photonic circuits  are changing rapidly.

Quantum computing, quantum networking, AI interconnects, and optical processing all depend on increasingly complex optical paths. At that scale, even small imperfections can limit system performance.

This is why the next phase of the industry will not be defined by a single device. It will be defined by the manufacturing platforms capable of supporting the entire quantum and optical computing stack.

In his latest post, our CEO, Ofer Shapiro,  shares why the race among photonic foundries is becoming one of the most important competitions in the quantum industry—and how Quantum Pulse Ventures is approaching this challenge.

#quantum #opticalprocessing #quantumcomputing

Every CEO from $LITE to $NVDA etc , is telling you that CPO is just around the corner and that it's going to be generational and inevitable

text:
Korea is putting US$1T+ behind #memory capex.

Everyone looks at the big equipment names: $AMAT $KLAC $LRCX.

Fair. BUT

The better question: who sits on the exact HBM chokepoint - less ran, cleaner, more elastic, and still checks the @aleabitoreddit playbook?

That’s why $FORM (#formfactor) is my #1 HBM pick-and-shovel name.

> Bottleneck strength: HIGH; every #HBM wafer needs to be probed before stacking; FORM sits at the test gate where yield risk gets caught early.

> Easy narrative: VERY HIGH; AI needs HBM -> HBM needs yield -> yield needs advanced probe cards. That is the whole pitch.

> Clean pure play: HIGH; advanced probe cards + wafer test exposure, without broad legacy semi-cap drag.

> Big 3 proximity: HIGH; direct exposure to #SKHynix / #Micron / #Samsung HBM ramps, with SK Hynix already naming FORM a “Best Partner.”

> Revenue speed: HIGH; FORM sits upstream of packaging; memory capex converts into orders earlier than many downstream HBM names.

> Growth runway: HIGH; Q1 FY26 already hit record revenue, while 2026–2028 rides HBM4, Texas capacity; estimate $1.6B revenue by 2030.

> Margin expansion: HIGH; gross margin is already near 49%; operating leverage supporting a path toward the 55% long-term target.

> MC elasticity: HIGH; ~$10B market cap; liquid enough for institutions, still small enough for real rerate. 

> Platform optionality: HIGH; HBM = first leg, #Foundry adds the second layer, and advanced packaging + #CPO/#photonics test can extend the story beyond the memory cycle. 

@jukan05 @ParadisLabs @ren_stocks @au_xbt @BULLOFBRITAIN

July Picks: Already Firing

$MU + $DRAM : Closing green. The AI memory trade remains strong. My view: $DRAM has a path to $100+ by late July or August if the memory rally continues.

$AAOI : $132 → $150
$OUST : $42 → $54

$IREN : Still the laggard. EOY target: $120+. July could see a move into the $70–80 range if momentum builds.

$CRDO : I believe this is setting up for a move into the $300s. After the recent pullback from the low $300s, strong revenue growth, AI networking demand, and partnerships across the photonics ecosystem could drive another leg higher.

I am STILL adding more $CRDO & $IREN at these levels.

text:
Researchers in the Bozhi Tian Lab have engineered a wireless, bioinspired "artificial leaf" that harvests light to power next-generation biomedical devices. Published in Nature Photonics, the study by lead author Pengju Li and co-first authors Mengzhan Liufu, Cooper R. Johnston, Young-Woo Pyo, Yuze Zheng, Guangqing Yang introduces a breakthrough nanoplasmonic material—composed of microscopic gold and titanium dioxide structures—that efficiently converts light into high-performance electrical energy. The team successfully used this wireless bioelectronic platform in animal models to pace heartbeats and stimulate sciatic nerves via external light activation, offering a powerful new avenue for target-specific medical therapies like nerve pain management. Beyond biostimulation, the material can even serve as a secure human-computer interface, allowing users to input information via invisible light that is instantly decoded by AI. Read the full story at - https://t.co/Jm0RhAe5VG

text:
$SIVE

Lightmatter CEO Nick Harris: “Lasers are incredibly important. They represent literally half of the revenue opportunity of all of CPO.”

He also expects the industry to move from near-package optics toward CPO over the next several years as copper reaches its physical limits. For a pure-play laser and photonics company like Sivers, this dramatically expands the strategic importance of its technology.

text:
Buzzberg Daily Alpha.

We scanned feed across X, YouTube and Reddit, Substack .

The cleanest signal was rotation into “next liquid proxies” after AI semis: space, defense drones, humanoid robotics, Korea AI capex, and select compute infrastructure.

1. Space / defense kept attracting the highest-quality flow.

$RKLB  was the strongest space narrative. The thesis is no longer just launches. Speakers framed Rocket Lab as the only public, full-stack space pure play, with the Iridium/spectrum angle creating optionality beyond D2D: IoT, maritime, telemetry, PNT, drones and defense.

$ASTS  was tied to J-LEO subsidy speculation and broader space momentum. $ONDS was framed as a long-term drone/defense compounder, with near-term dilution/warrant risk but Russell inclusion and possible strategic buyer optionality. Drone baskets like $UMAC/$KTOS/$AVAV also came up as “future of warfare” watchlist names.

2. Humanoid robotics moved beyond hype into public-market vehicles.

$CCXI  / Agility Robotics became the main robotics discussion. Bulls like the first US-listed humanoid pure-play angle, Amazon/Nvidia backing, ~$300M backlog, H2 2026 production scaling and RaaS unit economics.

The caution: some speakers openly dislike the SPAC quality/valuation but still expect a speculative run because the public-market wrapper is scarce.

$BOT also came up as a liquid way to own private robotics markups over the next 12-18 months.

3. Korea AI capex was the most important YouTube/macro signal.

Bloomberg and Korean YouTube focused on South Korea’s massive AI infrastructure plan: Samsung/SK Hynix, chip fabs, data centers, renewables and power equipment. The read-through is broad: memory, equipment, power grid, shipbuilding, and domestic Korean suppliers.

Twitter translated that into $MU/$SNDK/DRAM upside overnight. Korean channels also highlighted power equipment and shipbuilders as beneficiaries of AI data-center power shortages and government-backed capex.

4. AI compute relative value is back.

$PENG had the most interesting under-the-radar thesis: possible Middle East sovereign AI breadcrumbs through SK Telecom, MWC 2026 meetings, and Blackwell cluster integration.

$CRWV vs $NBIS was framed as a relative-value trade: CRWV allegedly trades at a large discount to NBIS on 2030 deployed-capacity revenue assumptions, while leverage concerns should fade if revenue scales.

$MSFT also reappeared as a “capex misunderstood” long: Reddit and Twitter both argued the market is penalizing AI buildout the same way it misread Amazon’s reinvestment cycle.

5. Select semis are separating from the weak photonics tape.

Broad optics/photonics was still treated cautiously, but $MXL and $QCOM stood out.

$MXL: Panther/OpenZFS validation at Los Alamos was cited as real proof for the CPU/storage offload thesis.

$QCOM: speakers highlighted automotive design wins, data-center revenue ramp, and HBC/near-memory compute as a lower-power inference architecture.

6. Single-name alpha:

$ABVX was the cleanest biotech catalyst: speakers argued the cancer/NMSC safety concern did not replicate, the label is cleaner, and UC efficacy supports a rerate.

$FCEL stayed alive as a speculative carbon-capture setup tied to the Exxon Rotterdam pilot. One speaker sold tactically to rotate into robotics, but the core FCEL thesis was not described as broken.

$BB had a strong QNX/software thread: autos, robotics, industrial automation, Canada/Japan trade mission deals, and high-margin software model.

$SMCI was the clearest avoid: Taiwan raids, chip-smuggling probe, accounting history, auditor issues. The message was simple: governance risk overwhelms AI exposure.

text:
$AXTI $COHR Do you believe this is last InP prepayment deal that will be cut over the next 5 years? We merely getting started.
———————-
BOTTOM LINE: This is incrementally bullish for COHR fundamentals but more strategically important than financially material. It confirms that 6-inch InP substrate supply remains a gating item for COHR’s AI optical ramp, validates AXT/Tongmei as a meaningful 6-inch InP substrate supplier, and shows COHR is using customer-style capacity prepayments upstream to lock supply through the next 3 years. It should be more stock-moving for AXTI than COHR, but for COHR it is a positive de-risking datapoint after a weak tape day, not a thesis-changing event by itself.

The agreement is straightforward but important. AXT-Tongmei entered a 3-year Master Development and Supply Agreement with Coherent, effective June 25, 2026, covering “mass development and supply” of 6-inch indium phosphide wafer substrates. AXT will expand capacity at its Beijing facility from 2026 through 2028, Coherent will make a $22.2885M prepayment for committed capacity, wafer prices are pre-established, and the prepayment will be applied against future product purchases. Coherent also gets refund rights if unused amounts remain at expiry or termination, and can terminate and recover unused prepayment if AXT fails to meet the capacity commitment for more than 6 successive months. AXT gets protection if Coherent misses minimum order quantities, in which case unused prepayment becomes nonrefundable and AXT can terminate. AXT must also offer additional capacity beyond the committed quantity to Coherent on the same terms. The full agreement will not be available until AXT files its Q2 2026 10-Q.  

The key interpretation is that this is not about outsourcing COHR’s differentiated InP device manufacturing. AXT is supplying 6-inch InP wafer substrates, the upstream crystalline substrate input. Coherent still does the value-added photonic device work: EMLs, CW lasers, photodiodes, PICs and related integration. That distinction matters because COHR’s moat remains process/yield/device manufacturing and vertical integration; AXT is enabling more wafer starts, not replacing COHR’s 6-inch InP fab advantage.

For COHR, the positive read is supply-chain de-risking. Management has repeatedly framed InP capacity as the key constraint in the AI optical cycle, while also saying 6-inch InP is central to the revenue and gross margin ramp. In the May earnings call, COHR said its 6-inch platform was producing EMLs, CW lasers and photodiodes, with yields exceeding 3-inch lines, first 6-inch-derived transceiver shipments already contributing to revenue and margin, and internal InP capacity expected to double ahead of plan and more than double again by the end of calendar 2027.   This AXT agreement strengthens confidence that substrate availability will not become the hidden bottleneck that prevents those internal wafer starts from scaling.

This also lines up with the J.P. Morgan investor-meeting takeaway that COHR had substrate supply “locked in” across 5 suppliers spanning multiple geographies, several capable of producing 6-inch substrates. The AXT filing effectively names 1 of those upstream supply arrangements and shows there is real money, fixed pricing, and committed capacity behind the company’s prior substrate comments.  

The less bullish read is that the deal confirms the bottleneck is real and persistent. Coherent would not be making take-or-pay-like commitments and prepaying a supplier for 3-year capacity if 6-inch InP substrates were abundant. Reuters recently reported that China’s InP export controls have disrupted the AI photonics supply chain, that AXT and Sumitomo dominate global InP substrate production, that AXT is a major supplier to Coherent, and that 6-inch InP wafer prices have reportedly risen sharply since China tightened export licensing.   The agreement helps COHR, but it does not eliminate geopolitical risk because the capacity expansion is at AXT’s Beijing facility, precisely the jurisdiction where export licensing and materials controls have become a live issue.

The deal is financially immaterial to COHR in isolation. COHR’s market cap is roughly $65B and the $22.3M prepayment is de minimis against the equity value, capex program, and cash balance. It is best viewed as a capacity reservation / working-capital deposit, likely recorded as a prepaid asset and then consumed through substrate purchases, not as an immediate P&L event. COHR was trading near $333 after a roughly -9.6% move on July 2, so the stock reaction is more likely to be driven by AI hardware sentiment, valuation, and semiconductor tape than by a $22M supplier prepayment.

For AXTI, however, the signal is materially stronger. AXT reported Q1 2026 revenue of $26.9M, so a $22.3M prepayment is roughly 83% of a single quarter’s reported revenue, even though it is not immediately recognized as revenue and the total contract value could be meaningfully different from the prepayment. AXT also said in April that it had completed a $632.5M capital raise to support Tongmei’s InP capacity expansion and 6-inch InP R&D, so the COHR contract provides named strategic-customer validation for that capital deployment.   AXTI was down roughly -12.9% on July 2, and this after-close filing could support a rebound when regular trading resumes.

The most important subtlety is that the $22.3M should not be treated as the total revenue opportunity. The filing says the prepayment is applied against purchases and ties to committed capacity; it does not disclose wafer volumes, per-wafer prices, shipment cadence, gross margin, or whether additional capacity is expected to be purchased beyond the initial commitment. Using Reuters’ cited $5,000 average 6-inch InP wafer price as a purely illustrative benchmark, the prepayment would equate to roughly 4,500 wafers, but that calculation should not be used as a volume estimate because actual contract pricing is confidential and the prepayment may only represent a portion of the broader commitment.  

The fixed-price element is meaningful. If spot 6-inch InP pricing continues to rise, Coherent has partially hedged substrate cost and capacity access. That matters for the gross margin bridge because COHR’s >42% non-GAAP gross margin path relies on 6-inch cost/yield benefits, higher 1.6T mix, internal sourcing, and better absorption. J.P. Morgan’s May conference note highlighted 42%+ gross margin tailwinds from the shift from 800G to higher-margin 1.6T, the transition from 3-inch to 6-inch wafers, and movement from external to internal sourcing.   The AXT agreement supports that framework, but the benefit likely shows up gradually through wafer starts, device output, module shipments, and margin conversion rather than immediately in the next quarter.

This is also a useful read-through to AI optical demand. Coherent is not just receiving long-term customer commitments; it is passing those commitments upstream. That is how a real supply-constrained hardware cycle behaves. Customers commit to COHR, COHR commits to AXT, AXT expands Beijing 6-inch capacity, and the entire chain becomes increasingly governed by deposits, capacity reservations, fixed prices, minimum orders, and termination protections. This reinforces the view that the AI optical cycle is capacity constrained rather than purely speculative order inflation, though it does not disprove some double-ordering risk.

The agreement also has a China-risk wrinkle that investors should not ignore. Coherent has emphasized U.S. manufacturing scale, Sherman 6-inch InP leadership, CHIPS-related support, and the NVIDIA strategic partnership. This AXT agreement reveals that even a U.S.-anchored 6-inch InP device strategy still depends on upstream substrate inputs from China. That is not inherently negative, because AXT is a scaled and strategically important substrate supplier, but it means export permits, U.S.-China policy, force majeure language, and qualification of non-China supply remain critical diligence items.

Relative implications are clear. For COHR, this is a positive data point for execution confidence and supply visibility, but not enough on its own to revise estimates. For AXTI, it is a much bigger validation event because it confirms that a leading AI optical platform company is willing to prepay for Tongmei 6-inch substrate capacity. For LITE, the read-through is mixed: positive for the industry because InP scarcity is validated, but potentially negative at the margin if COHR is locking a larger share of scarce AXT 6-inch output. For the broader optical ecosystem, the deal supports the view that the scarcity value sits not only in finished transceivers but also deep upstream in compound semiconductor substrates, lasers, isolators, PM fiber, ELS modules, and other enabling components.

Trading implication: this should help stabilize the COHR narrative after the July 2 selloff, but it is not large enough to single-handedly re-rate the stock. The better near-term setup is probably AXTI up more sharply on customer validation and COHR modestly supported, with the caveat that U.S. markets are closed Friday, July 3 for Independence Day observed, so regular-session price discovery is delayed.   COHR remains a high-conviction structural AI optical beneficiary, but the stock still needs gross margin progression, 6-inch output conversion, and FCF inflection to justify the valuation. This announcement improves confidence in the supply-chain bridge to that outcome.

Key diligence questions for Monday/next week: whether this AXT capacity was already included in COHR’s “5 substrate suppliers” commentary or is incremental; whether export licenses are already secured; what share of FY2027 6-inch wafer starts this covers; whether the agreement includes first-priority capacity rights or merely committed capacity; whether the fixed wafer prices are below current spot; how quickly AXT can qualify volume 6-inch wafers to COHR specifications; and whether any of the prepayment is ultimately customer-funded through COHR’s own strategic LTAs.

text:
🔔 The Daily Semi-Cap — July 2, 2026

📊 SMH: $592.29 (-4.54%)

📈 Earnings:
• No new semiconductor earnings released today.

🔥 Major Developments:
• AXT-Tongmei signed a three-year 6-inch InP wafer substrate capacity agreement with Coherent, including a $22.3M prepayment tied to expanded Beijing capacity from 2026-2028. The optics supply chain is moving from spot-market scramble to locked capacity, which matters for Coherent, AXT, and the broader AI photonics bottleneck.
• Anthropic is exploring a custom AI chip with Samsung as a potential manufacturing partner, evaluating Samsung’s 2nm process and advanced packaging. If it moves forward, the read-through is bigger than one AI lab: frontier model companies are trying to control silicon roadmaps, and Samsung is still hunting for a marquee AI foundry win against TSMC.
• Nvidia is offering smaller GPU cloud providers financial backstops and revenue-share structures tied to unused GPU capacity. That supports near-term accelerator demand, but it also makes the AI infrastructure stack more circular: chip demand, cloud financing, and end-customer utilization are becoming increasingly intertwined.
• Meta said AI agent development over the last four months has not accelerated as expected, adding pressure to the capex ROI debate after reports that it may sell excess AI compute capacity. The takeaway is not that AI demand is dead; it is that hyperscaler capex now needs cleaner monetization proof, not just bigger buildout numbers.

text:
Hello darkness my old friend. 

Right when I think we’re done, you find a way to lure me back in!

I made peace with cutting $AXTI yesterday and it somehow finds a way to drag me back in. 

This deal is huge and the pre payment alone is nearly as much as their entire Q1 revenue. 

Not only that but it’s the catalyst it needs to shake off the recent fear around the AI infra trade and specifically photonics. 

This is my fifth time trying to trade AXTI in the past month. Is this time different?

text:
Fiber & Infrastructure Highlights – July 2, 2026

Conexon Connect Georgia completed its ninth FTTH project with Satilla REMC, surpassing 240,000 homes/businesses, 5,200 fiber miles, and delivering multi-gig to ~60,000 locations across 9 counties. Vocus announced ~A$500M ducted Sydney-Melbourne long-haul (6,912 cores, ready 2029, AI-driven). Cloud Capital/Realty Income formed a $6B data center JV (Virginia). South Korea outlined $919B mega projects with 18.4GW data centers by 2035. Nixxy/Tachyon announced a 1GW North Dakota campus. Tata advanced India-Singapore subsea.

Ongoing AI hyperscaler demand drives fiber supply ramps (Corning, STL, CommScope, Clearfield, Fujikura tripling production) amid persistent shortages (preforms tight through 2026+). Regional OSP/ISP activity includes Ziply Northwest, Ripple Fiber WA, Ezee Fiber, Brightspeed WiFi 7, Burlington Telecom 5 Gbps, Heartland Fiber Project, FiberLight West Texas, Big Fiber financing, Atomic Fiber Los Alamos, and various BEAD/FTTH builds. Manufacturing focuses on high-count, multi-core, photonics; FBA whitepaper on fiber-AI convergence and power/grid constraints add momentum.

 #FiberForge #FiberOptics #OSPISP

text:
【量子ハードウェア】
Pasqalがカナダで光集積回路（PIC）実装拠点を新設。790万ドル（約11.9億円、1ドル=150円換算）規模で量子・センシング向け製造基盤を強化。
（2026年7月3日 日本時間、Quantum Computing Report）

何が起きたか：
Pasqalは、買収したカナダ子会社Aeponyxを通じて、ケベック州ブロモンのC2MI（MiQro Innovation Collaborative Center）内に、光集積回路（PIC）パッケージングの専門拠点を設立すると発表した。 

このプロジェクトは総額790万ドル（約11.9億円、1ドル=150円換算）で、うち400万ドル（約6.0億円）は連邦・州政府が支援し、その中にはNGen（Next Generation Manufacturing Canada）からの300万ドル（約4.5億円）が含まれる。 

拠点では、シリコンナイトライド（SiN）フォトニクスを活用したPICの実装・組立ラインを構築し、レーザー制御モジュールなど量子コンピュータや量子センシングに必要な光部品の量産体制を整える。初期は試作・少量生産を担い、将来的には年間50万個超のモジュール生産能力を目指す計画だ。HOP Technologies、Phantom Photonicsも参画し、医療センシングやLiDARなど量子以外の用途も視野に入れる。 

読み解き：
今回のポイントは「量子コンピュータを作る」ことではなく、「量子コンピュータを量産できる部品産業を作る」ことにある。

量子コンピュータではチップそのもの以上に、レーザー、光導波路、ファイバー接続などの光学実装が製造ボトルネックになりやすい。特に中性原子方式は多数のレーザーを高精度で制御する必要があり、PICの高品質な実装技術は性能だけでなく生産性にも直結する。 

興味深いのは、この拠点が量子専用工場ではなく、防衛向けLiDARや医療センシング企業も巻き込んだ共通インフラとして設計されている点だ。同じフォトニクス製造設備を複数産業で共有できれば、量子産業単独では難しい設備投資の採算性を高められる。近年は各国が「量子主権」を意識したサプライチェーン整備を進めているが、この発表も製造能力そのものを国内に確保する流れの一例と言える。Pasqalにとっても、Aeponyx買収後の垂直統合を具体的な製造基盤へ発展させる動きとして位置付けられる。 

評価：
・業界インパクト：★★★
・注目度：★★★
・関連銘柄：PASQAL（未上場）

結論：
量子業界ではプロセッサ性能だけでなく、「安定して作れる製造基盤」が競争力になり始めている。今後は各社が光部品やパッケージング、製造拠点への投資をどこまで拡大するかが、商用展開の速度を左右する重要な指標になりそうだ。

出典：Quantum Computing Report
(https://t.co/kVpXEuzfHj)

text:
【研究開発】
米NSFが量子フォトニクス実用化へ400万ドル追加支援。GPS不要の量子ナビゲーションを視野に、最終段階では最大5,500万ドル規模の量子センター構想へ発展する可能性。
（2026年7月3日 〔日本時間〕・The Quantum Insider）

何が起きたか：
米ミシガン大学が主導する量子フォトニクス研究プロジェクト「QuPID（Quantum Photonic Integration and Deployment）」が、米国立科学財団（NSF）のNational Quantum Virtual Laboratory（NQVL）プログラムのフェーズ2に採択され、2年間で400万ドル（約6億円、1ドル=150円換算）の助成を受けることになった。これはフェーズ1の100万ドル（約1.5億円）に続く資金で、将来的には最大5,500万ドル（約82.5億円）のNSF量子センターへ発展する可能性がある。研究では、量子状態の光を利用した集積フォトニクスチップを開発し、GPSが使えない水中・宇宙・月面などでも利用できる高精度ナビゲーションや超高精度センシングを目指す。プロジェクトにはオハイオ州立大学、ハーバード大学、スタンフォード大学、パデュー大学のほか、Honeywell、TOPTICA Photonics、General Motors、Toyota、Raytheon、NASA Glenn Research Center、Air Force Research Laboratoryなど産学官が参加している。 

読み解き：
このニュースの本質は、「量子コンピュータ」ではなく「量子フォトニクス」の商用化に研究資金の重心が移っている点にある。今回の目標は演算性能ではなく、量子光学を使った実環境向けセンサーやナビゲーションであり、量子技術をインフラへ組み込む流れを象徴している。

特にNSFがフェーズ制で資金を拡大し、最終的には数千万ドル規模の量子センターを構想していることは、単なる大学研究ではなく、量産・実装を前提としたエコシステム形成を狙っていることを示す。さらに、自動車メーカー、防衛企業、NASAまで参加していることから、出口市場が明確な研究テーマとして位置付けられている点も重要だ。

量子業界では計算機だけでなく、量子センシングや量子通信が商用化を先行する可能性が高いと見られており、今回の支援はその流れを裏付ける動きといえる。 

評価：
・業界インパクト：★★★
・注目度：★★★
・関連銘柄：HON, GM, TM

結論：
量子技術の競争軸は計算能力だけではなく、センシングやナビゲーションなど現実世界の用途へ広がりつつある。今後は、このような研究プロジェクトが実証実験や政府調達へ発展するかどうかが、商用化を見極める重要なポイントになる。

embedded_text: Meta’s $META Zuckerberg says AI development hasn’t accelerated in way we expected, per Bloomberg

text:
The Market That Changed Engines Mid‑Flight Strategic & Tactical Read of the Day 

Today's tape told a story that wasn't subtle: the market didn't break, it pivoted. Momentum the great engine of the 2025–H1 2026 rally finally lost altitude. Semiconductors, memory, photonics, and the AI‑power complex all slipped into a decisive unwind. META admitted AI progress has been slower than expected; photonics rejected their 21‑DMA; semis logged a second straight 5%+ selloff; MTUM printed its sixth rare >3% down day while RSP rose. That's not noise that's regime change.

Yet beneath the surface, the bull market kept breathing. Defensive sectors surged: healthcare, staples, utilities, materials. Equal‑weight advanced. The Dow hit a record. Investors didn't de‑risk they reallocated. This is classic bull‑market rotation: money staying in equities, but choosing different leaders.

Macro reinforced the shift. Payrolls softened, real earnings remain pressured, and factory orders ex‑transport were solid a mix that tempers rate‑hike fears without signaling recession. Micro confirmed it: hyperscalers stabilizing, domestic cyclicals strengthening, and median earnings tracking double‑digit growth. The bottleneck trade is crowded; the broader market is not.

Strategically, leadership is migrating from AI‑momentum to diversified, cash‑flow‑rich, domestically leveraged sectors. Tactically, expect digestion in semis and selective strength in defensives until Q2 earnings clarify whether AI re‑accelerates or hands the baton to the rest of the market.

Bull markets don't die when leaders tire they evolve when new ones emerge.

And to all American readers: Happy Independence Day may your portfolios enjoy some fireworks too. 🇺🇸✨

Following advanced training in Germany on the Heidelberg DWL66+ system by SNF staff members Sruthi Kuriakose and Angela Tanesha, SNF is expanding expertise in 3D grayscale lithography, enabling complex microstructures for future research in photonics, quantum technologies, sensing and micro-optics.

text:
WPI’s Core Research Facilities provide shared access to advanced instrumentation—from nano-fabrication and microscopy to photonics, imaging, and medical device testing—powering research across many disciplines. 

Learn more about how you can become a part of our research community here: https://t.co/AhKDDoIZEx.

我写过 Soitec、SIVE、IQE、Shunsin、AAOI……很多人把它们当成各自独立的票。但如果你把这些环节摊在一张桌子上,会发现一个几乎没人问过的问题:它们最后,是在谁的工厂里被"连起来"造出来的?

今天我们来聊一下这家快被遗忘的欧洲小厂: $XFAB的故事。 先说几个我一直都觉得很有意思的点:

1. 首先它的出身就带有历史性意味，它是 1992 年从"东德解体后被拆分的电子工业巨头"里长出来的, 然后被一批比利时人接手, 而这批比利时人还顺手创办了另一家汽车芯片龙头 Melexis，这意味着同一批人,一手造芯片、一手造工厂。

2. 它不卷纳米, 却能够把英伟达和诺基亚拉进了自己的局。 它牵头的一个欧盟出钱的硅光子项目中英伟达、诺基亚、imec 都是同桌的参与方。 一家欧洲小代工厂,能召集起这种阵容,本身就说明问题。

3. 它可能是我这一整条硅光/CPO 链的"总枢纽"。 它在 Soitec 的 SOI 地基上、用 SMART Photonics 的 InP,造出喂给 AAOI 的硅光芯片;同时又是 NVTS 那条"电"线上的 SiC 代工龙头，一只手 AI 光、一只手 EV 电。 

但真正让我觉得很神奇的点在于它约六成的股份,锁在两个创始家族和一个州政府手里，但我们需要知道为什么是这样？而这样又意味着什么？

text:
I'm seeing it every day. 

Investors comparing their YTD, YoY performance, and even weekly/daily performance. 

It's really not fair to compare apples and pumpkins. 

- Investor investing in cyclical companies will definitely go up and down based on the cycle and timing.
- Investor investing in high growth will perform during low rates but struggle during inflation.
- Investor investing in value will miss huge runs but they focus on safety.
- Investing in biotech, pharma, car, energy industry all brings different outcomes. If car industry is lagging, it's impossible to expect an SME for this industry to find 10 baggers in photonics. 

That's just one piece of the story. 

The next one is about your investing style or strategy.
- Long?
- Day/swing trading? 
- Using options? 
- Using all of the above? 

In a volatile environment swing trader should have more options and long investors may underperform. If the market has bearish outlook, shorters may have more luck than the rest?

We can't compare traders in robotics vs long term investors in consumer staples. Makes no sense. 

There's the last piece of the story. 

Not everyone is having the same amount of cash available, nor the same risk tolerance, nor the same time horizon, nor time commitment.  

One investor has a difficult work and no time to pay attention to daily movements. He can't really go and day trade or swing trade. 
Another one with 5 billion portfolio cannot have 50% in a single month. He is likely happy with 3%. 
A student having 200 usd to throw in $SIVE and getting 5x cannot be compared to someone managing his family portfolio. 

When you next time compare yourself with someone, make sure to understand these three dimensions. 

It's VERY likely the guy you're comparing yourself with has different investing profile and strategy. Maybe he isn't investing in memory and buying selling every day. 

Only compare your current performance to your own last month or last year. 

Competing with oneself is one of the most effective paths to sustained personal growth in anything you do.

TBB

It's really not fair to compare apples and pumpkins. - Investor investing in cyclical companies will definitely go up and down based on the cycle and timing. - Investor investing in high growth will perform during low rates but struggle during inflation. - Investor investing in value will miss huge runs but they focus on safety. - Investing in biotech, pharma, car, energy industry all brings different outcomes. We can't compare traders in robotics vs long term investors in consumer staples. Makes no sense. There's the last piece of the story. Not everyone is having the same amount of cash available, nor the same risk tolerance, nor the same time horizon, nor time commitment. One investor has a difficult work and no time to pay attention to daily movements. He can't really go and day trade or swing trade. Another one with 5 billion portfolio cannot have 50% in a single month. He is likely happy with 3%. A student having 200 usd to throw in $SIVE and getting 5x cannot be compared to someone managing his family portfolio. When you next time compare yourself with someone, make sure to understand these three dimensions. It's VERY likely the guy you're comparing yourself with has different investing profile and strategy. text:
Mapping $SIVE 2030:

Current: ~52 SEK 

2030 Target: 260 SEK 

Cap: ~85B SEK

The catalyst is the AI Power Wall. As copper interconnects fail, Sivers photonics becomes the standard for data center light sources. A pure play on the transition from hardware to AI infrastructure.

For $SIVE, the 2030 re-rating is a bet on the "Optical Transition." Right now, the market sees them as a niche small-cap component maker. By 2030, they have the potential to be the standard-bearer for light-source technology in AI clusters.
Here is why I believe the bull case for a ~260 SEK target is realistic:

1.The Silicon Photonics (SiPh) Shift: Data centers are hitting a "Power Wall." Traditional copper wires generate too much heat and lose too much signal at AI scale. Moving data with light (photonics) is 1,000x more efficient. Sivers Photonics produces the high-power DFB lasers that act as the "engine" for these optical interconnects.

2.The LEO Satellite Explosion: Beyond AI, their beamforming chips are core to the Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite market. As global internet coverage shifts to space, the demand for low-cost, high-efficiency ground and satellite terminals will move from thousands to millions of units.

3.The 6G Infrastructure Bridge: As we move toward 2030, the 6G standard will rely heavily on mmWave technology. Sivers has been refining this tech for years while others were focused only on standard 5G. They own the "hard part" of the spectrum.
If they successfully navigate the liquidity hurdles of the next few years, the "massive gap" you see is the market finally pricing them as a critical infrastructure provider rather than a speculative hardware play.

text:
Mapping the 2030 landscape: the gap between current prices and future market caps for $AAOI, $MU, and $SNDK is massive.

$AAOI: $135 to $1,200 

$MU: $1,060 to $3,200 

$SNDK: $1,880 to $4,800

The tech backbone is being fundamentally re-valued. This is asymmetry in action.

The "Why" behind the 2030 targets:

1.$AAOI (The Photonics Pivot): The move from 400G to 800G and eventually 1.6T connectivity is the massive catalyst here. As AI clusters grow, the "plumbing" (optical transceivers) becomes the primary bottleneck. We are looking at a fundamental re-rating from a hardware supplier to a critical AI infrastructure provider.

2.$MU (The HBM Standard): High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) has changed the game. Memory is no longer a cyclical commodity; it's a dedicated AI component with much higher margins. By 2030, $MU won't just be selling chips, they will be selling the "oxygen" that keeps AI GPUs running.

3.$SNDK (Enterprise Data Lakes): As companies move from training AI to running inference on massive datasets, the demand for enterprise-grade NAND (storage) will explode. $SNDK is positioned to capture the shift toward high-density, low-power storage required for 2030-era data centers.

text:
🔌 Copper interconnects inside AI data centers are hitting a wall.

At 800G+, copper's resistive losses and heat make it physically unscalable for anything beyond a few meters. The industry is already shifting scale-out links to optics — Broadcom, Nvidia, and TSMC are all shipping co-packaged optics in 2026. Scale-up (inside the rack) is next in line.

Optical interconnect market: ~$16B in 2024 → projected $34-41B by 2030.

The stock map across the photonics stack:

🔹 Layer 1 — Materials & wafers
$GLW $AXTI $IQE $AIXA $AMS
🔹 Layer 2 — Core photonic devices
$IPGP $COHR $LITE $LASR $SIVE
🔹 Layer 3 — Components & modules
$AAOI $MTSI $FN $VIAV $LPTH
🔹 Layer 4 — Systems & equipment
$ASML $BESI $ASM $LPKF $MKS
🔹 Layer 5 — Test, metrology & yield
$CAMT $FORM $AEHR $ONTO $VIAV

Reality check: this isn't "copper is dead." Industry consensus at OFC 2026 is heterogeneity — copper still wins scale-up for now, optics is winning scale-out. But most of these names already ran hard this year and are extremely volatile on any single headline.

Not financial advice — just mapping the theme.

Which layer has the most room left, or is this already priced in?

#Photonics #AI #Semiconductors #DataCenters

text:
Yeah, SemiAnalysis dropped a bearish photonics note on June 9, and about 3 weeks later they co-launched an ETF that holds $LITE. Hard to say it’s not intentional.

This is why I’d rather build conviction from my own work than trust a note that an analysis company posted. They act in their own interests.

text:
UDN Money report "殺翻光通訊後 SemiAnalysis 惹議 業界揭其劣跡 許多公司慘遭魔手":

- SemiAnalysis, released a report in June, questioning the timing of the launch of the CPO on the grounds of low yield and delay, which caused the photonics sector to collapse. 

- The agency then partnered with Tema to launch a photonics ETF. 

- The shares held in the ETF under the trading code LAZR happened to be the photonics stocks that were mentioned negatively in the report

$HIMX and $LITE, which were specifically targeted negatively in their article "Powered Down, Lights Off", have been bought in Semianalysis and Tema's new ETF after the optical sector crash.

Tower’s Q1 earnings call disclosed that Oriole is using PH18DA, Tower’s advanced silicon photonics platform featuring heterogeneously integrated indium phosphide (InP) optical amplifiers.

This wasn’t highlighted in the original press release.

Back in March, Tower Semiconductor announced that Oriole had selected its silicon photonics platform for PRISM.

Tower highlighted integrated:

• lasers
• optical amplification
• switching
• modulation
• detection

text:
blog's up 👇

short version - chips stopped shrinking, so the money moved from making silicon to packaging it. in silicon photonics, packaging is ~60-70% of the chip.

india spent $18bn on semis and spent almost all of it in commodity OSAT - the low margin part. the high-value, advanced packaging + photonics - has no listed indian pure-play in India.

except one - a small auto-software company from bangalore claiming india's only photonics packaging line - the tech story is genuinely interesting considering how they have build the capablity but the governance file is kinda scary.

not a reco. just us following the value chain.

https://t.co/PTZxygiWPR

we've been reading a lot about advanced packaging (thanks to citrini year's start blog) and how we can play it via Indian players. 

we are pretty much stuck at OSAT on ground level given we have been a little* behind in terms of getting those capablities (a little😅)

text:
#IZMO 
Silicon Photonics (SiPh) represents a massive fundamental shift in how data moves, driven by the structural bottlenecks of artificial intelligence workloads.

1. The Silicon Photonics Market: Why the Indirection Matters
Historically, microchips communicated internally and externally using electrons traveling over copper wires. However, as high-performance GenAI training clusters, large language models (LLMs), and hyperscale data centers expand, copper has hit a physical brick wall known as RC delay and skin effect. At extreme speeds, copper wires generate unsustainable heat, consume excessive power, and suffer severe signal degradation.

Silicon Photonics replaces electrons with photons (light). By fabricating optical waveguides directly onto silicon substrates, chips can transmit data using lasers.

Main Market Verticals (2026 Trajectory)
AI Clusters & Datacom: The primary growth engine. Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) fuse optical engines right next to the compute ASIC/GPU, expanding transceiver bandwidth past 1.6T and 3.2T.

Aerospace & Defense: High-frequency Electronic Warfare (EW), phased-array radar signal processing, and space payloads where weight reduction and immunity to electromagnetic interference (EMI) are critical.

Quantum Computing: Distributing entangled photons across processing nodes via low-loss optical interconnects.

Major Indian Competitors 
India’s domestic chip landscape is heavily skewed toward legacy assembly, testing, and marking (ATMP) or standard wire-bonded packaging. True Silicon Photonics capability is highly restricted

IZMO’s Specific Role in the Value Chain: The Packaging Moat
In Silicon Photonics, packaging is the ultimate commercial bottleneck, routinely accounting for 60% to 80% of the total component cost.

While aligning electrical microbumps allows for minor margins of error, aligning an optical fiber to a silicon chip's internal sub-micron waveguide requires nanometer-scale precision. A misalignment of even a few hundred nanometers can cause catastrophic insertion loss, rendering the optical link useless.

Through its subsidiary, izmomicro (based in Whitefield, Bengaluru), Izmo has deliberately chosen not to compete in the high-cap-ex, low-margin foundry or standard assembly market. Instead, it has positioned itself as a specialized, high-barrier Advanced Packaging Partner.

The Silicon Photonics Market: Why the Indirection Matters Historically, microchips communicated internally and externally using electrons traveling over copper wires. However, as high-performance GenAI training clusters, large language models (LLMs), and hyperscale data centers expand, copper has hit a physical brick wall known as RC delay and skin effect. At extreme speeds, copper wires generate unsustainable heat, consume excessive power, and suffer severe signal degradation. Silicon Photonics replaces electrons with photons (light). By fabricating optical waveguides directly onto silicon substrates, chips can transmit data using lasers. Main Market Verticals (2026 Trajectory) AI Clusters & Datacom: The primary growth engine. Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) fuse optical engines right next to the compute ASIC/GPU, expanding transceiver bandwidth past 1.6T and 3.2T. Aerospace & Defense: High-frequency Electronic Warfare (EW), phased-array radar signal processing, and space payloads where weight reduction and immunity to electromagnetic interference (EMI) are critical. Quantum Computing: Distributing entangled photons across processing nodes via low-loss optical interconnects. Major Indian Competitors India’s domestic chip landscape is heavily skewed toward legacy assembly, testing, and marking (ATMP) or standard wire-bonded packaging. True Silicon Photonics capability is highly restricted IZMO’s Specific Role in the Value Chain: The Packaging Moat In Silicon Photonics, packaging is the ultimate commercial bottleneck, routinely accounting for 60% to 80% of the total component cost. While aligning electrical microbumps allows for minor margins of error, aligning an optical fiber to a silicon chip's internal sub-micron waveguide requires nanometer-scale precision. A misalignment of even a few hundred nanometers can cause catastrophic insertion loss, rendering the optical link useless. Through its subsidiary, izmomicro (based in Whitefield, Bengaluru), Izmo has deliberately chosen not to compete in the high-cap-ex, low-margin foundry or standard assembly market. Instead, it has positioned itself as a specialized, high-barrier Advanced Packaging Partner."}}}, "rest_id": "2073443344861282413", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "337", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Everyone is asking which AI company will win.

I think that is the wrong question.

The real question is:

who sells the physical bottlenecks that every AI winner will be forced to consume?

Compute.
Energy.
Grid.
Cooling.
Semiconductors.
Photonics.
Silicon.
Uranium.
HALEU.
Isotopes.
Nuclear fuel.
Advanced materials.
AI may not be a bubble.
But most AI companies will die.
The internet changed the world, but most dot-com stocks disappeared.
The same can happen again.
That is why I am not only watching the obvious names like NVDA, TSM, ASML and AVGO.
I am watching the invisible infrastructure:
ASPI — isotopes, Silicon-28, HALEU
LEU — uranium enrichment / HALEU
CEG — nuclear power
BWXT — nuclear components
VRT — power and cooling
ETN — electrical infrastructure
PWR — grid construction
GEV — grid and energy systems
ALAB — AI connectivity
COHR / LITE — photonics
BESI — advanced packaging
SMR / OKLO — advanced nuclear
FLNC / POWL / AMSC — grid, storage and power equipment
ORA / FRVO — geothermal firm power
GFUZ / TAE — fusion as the long-duration frontier
I may be early.
I may be wrong on some names.
But I refuse to only chase the application layer while the real war is moving into electricity, materials, nuclear fuel, interconnects and supply chains.
The future of AI will not be decided only by models.

It will be decided by the physical infrastructure that allows intelligence to exist at scale.

I am not looking for hype.

I am looking for bottlenecks. Bye . #AIBottlenecks

#AIInfrastructure #EnergyWar

I was scrolling trough my feed today, and the only thing I see is people comparing Quantum (2025) to photonics. 

Yeah we seen a very good run for quantum and then it just dumped. But I know why it dumped. Because THERE WAS NO REAL REVENUE. 

Comparing to photonics names like $AAOI they have real revenue and huge projections. So don’t compare some temporary bs to something real.

text:
$COHR $LITE $AAOI 

The recent hammering of photonics/CPO names, which was partly triggered by the SemiAnalysis note pushing timelines out for CPO adoption, looks like a short-sighted overreaction rather than a collapse in the fundamental thesis imo.

The realistic path is to use copper for scale-up as long as you can on short-reach, low-latency links inside the rack/cluster, and then move to optics as the final phase of the transition. The move to optics is fundamentally necessary. Copper interconnects eventually hit physical limits w/ exponentially rising power draw, signal degradation, and restricted reach as AI clusters scale to massive sizes w/ extreme bandwidth demands. Copper simply cannot efficiently move data across longer distances and at the densities required w/o consuming unsustainable power or compromising performance. 

This hybrid, phased reality is not a bear case. Copper staying relevant longer actually extends the runway and de-risks CPO adoption as clusters scale to a point where copper hits hard walls.

We’re still early in the multi-year AI infrastructure buildout. The “slow ramp” narrative today is exactly what you should be expect anyways before the volumes compound in the back half of the decade.

text:
Dear @vonderleyen,
How wonderful that you and the entire College of Commissioners travelled to Cork today to visit the Tyndall National Institute! A real “innovation powerhouse,” as you so aptly put it. “From test to scale, from ideas to industry” – that sounds like proper European future music. And Ireland really is a beautiful country. The green hills, the Guinness, the friendly people… a business trip like that must be quite enjoyable. The European taxpayer is surely delighted that you’re all so motivated and on the road.
Just one small, modest question out of pure fiscal curiosity: Did you know that right on your doorstep in Leuven, Belgium, there is an institute that is considered the global benchmark in exactly this field – integrated semiconductors, nanoelectronics, photonics and deep-tech hardware?
imec employs over 6,500 scientists, engineers and innovators from more than 100 countries. That’s more than ten times the size of Tyndall (which had a proud 580 staff and students in 2024, including 173 postgraduates with 158 PhD students). imec was Belgium’s top patent applicant in 2024/2025 and is driving major European Chips Act pilot lines. While Tyndall is getting an expansion worth over €100 million and works with around 200 industry partners, imec has had a global network for years and generates well over a billion euros in revenue annually – mostly through direct industry collaborations.
But of course: why stay in Leuven when you can fly the whole College, security teams, protocol staff and all the rest to Ireland instead? The taxpayers in Germany, France, Italy and all the other member states are happy to pay for a bit of extra European unity – plus flights, hotels, escorted convoys and the entire logistical effort for a visit to Cork, when Leuven is only about a half-hour drive from Brussels.
We really do think it’s nice that you praise Tyndall so much. The institute has been doing good work since it was founded in 2004 (with roots in the NMRC from 1981). But if you’re going to celebrate European top-tier research, then please do it where it’s biggest, most established and closest. It would save money – and CO₂. The Irish government can still be happy anyway – they’ve already received plenty of EU funds.
With friendly but slightly puzzled regards,

text:
Disorder creates new properties in compound semiconductors - Helmholtz-Zentrum Berlin (HZB) https://t.co/YcmkNFjBWE

This first clear demonstration of phonon-exciton asymmetry opens doors to next-gen optoelectronics. The material pretends to be a bland cubic citizen but reveals a sophisticated, directionally picky inner life under light. Revolutionary for energy tech and photonics, disorder as deliberate design tool. Schorr’s team calls it “an additional handle for tailoring optoelectronic properties.”

Talk about a stick save  $LITE 

You got that potential U&R (Undercut and Recover) on the 100 day for 

Should be an interesting rest of week for this name and many other photonics names ex $GLW which is living in its own world.  

Have they shook out all the new comers and they are ready for a new leg?  Or is this Lucy waving the football at Charlie Brown just to pull it away. 

No one knows, but I'll have the 🍿 out

text:

This is where silicon photonics and micro-ring resonators become important.

A micro-ring can act like a tiny wavelength-selective filter.

It can help select, modulate, or separate specific wavelengths.

That makes WDM easier to integrate into photonic chips.

text:
This is the right take → attention relative to float is a fragility gauge, not a buy signal. The crowding z-score peaks right before the air pocket.

What's worth adding → it's symmetric, and your own example is confirming it in real time. The radar caught flow rotating OUT of photonics. Photonics is bouncing today. The same sensor that flagged crowding fragility on the way up flags abandonment on the way down → attention decays, the float stays pinned, and the setup reloads.

Crowding fragile, neglect cheap, same indicator read in two directions. Your Burry-SOXX fun fact is the purest version of that → peak query is peak neglect is the bottom. 👀

text:
$SIVE and $AAPL. Funny how a company named Rockley led us here.

A lot of you are asking if this connection is confirmed. Let me break it down.

Rockley Photonics was Apple's laser supplier for their glucose monitoring project. Apple poured $70 million into them.

Rockley went bankrupt in 2023.

So where does $AAPL go next?

Sivers Semiconductors is a small Swedish company that makes extremely precise lasers. So precise that they are the only laser technology in the world that can measure blood glucose through your skin without a needle.

That is not marketing. That is physics.

Here is what is officially confirmed.

In January 2024 $SIVE published a legal regulatory filing. The kind where executives face criminal charges if they lie.

It states that a US Fortune 100 company has been working with $SIVE for over 5 years. Paid $17 million in development fees. And just placed an order for 50,000 laser devices for a wearable health product.

That is real. That is in the filing.

The timing lines up. The technology fits. The unit volumes match.

$AAPL is never named in any $SIVE document. That part is still inference.

But if it is confirmed casually run these numbers.
Apple Watch ships up to 54 million units a year. At $10 to $15 per laser that is $500 to $750 million in potential annual revenue for $SIVE. Comparable sole source Apple suppliers have historically traded at 25 to 40 times sales at peak.

$SIVE sits at a $1.9 billion market cap today.
Rockley bankruptcy. $AAPL needs a new laser supplier. $SIVE announces a Fortune 100 wearable customer.

The confirmed volume order is the catalyst that changes everything.

text:
The silicon photonics supply chain diagram is one of the most important investment maps in technology right now (Save this).

Every AI data center being built depends on what's in that chart and the companies inside it are just starting to show what the revenue ramp looks like.

Here are the 10 stocks I am watching.

1. Broadcom (AVGO): Broadcom's AI revenue rocketed 106% to $8.4 billion in its most recent quarter, driven by custom accelerator chips for Google, Meta, and Anthropic, plus AI networking silicon. It is one of the few companies doing both the switch silicon and the photonics integration needed for co-packaged optics at hyperscale. The silicon photonics diagram shows Broadcom appearing across multiple layers, PIC design, laser sources, photodiode, and EIC design.

2. MACOM Technology (MTSI): MACOM is quietly positioning itself at the inflection point of the 1.6T and 3.2T optical transceiver transition. In March 2026 it launched 448G PAM4 modulator drivers among the first in the industry and joined Broadcom, Cisco, and Semtech in the 400G Optical MSA standards consortium. In June 2026 it introduced hot via chip scale technology, eliminating wire bonds in its AlGaAs packaging.

3. Marvell Technology (MRVL): Marvell is the DSP engine inside many of the optical modules in that chart. Its custom AI silicon, built for hyperscalers who want to own their accelerator architecture is the fastest growing part of its business. It sits at the EIC design layer and supplies the digital signal processing that makes high-speed optical links work reliably at scale. As the industry shifts from 400G to 800G and 1.6T, every step requires more sophisticated DSPs and Marvell captures more revenue per module shipped.

4. Keysight Technologies (KEYS): Every optical module that ships from every fab in that chart has to be tested before it reaches a data center. Keysight is the global leader in electronic and photonic test equipment. As optical speeds push to 800G and beyond, the testing instrumentation has to keep pace and it commands premium pricing.

5. FormFactor (FORM): FormFactor is the most underappreciated name in this entire chart. Q1 2026 revenue was $226 million, up 32% year over year, beating estimates with non-GAAP EPS of $0.56 versus $0.44 expected. It acquired Keystone Photonics in December 2025, becoming the leading wafer-level silicon photonics test platform for co-packaged optics production. Its partnership with Advantest created the world's fastest automated photonic alignment test system with nine-axis nano-precision. Over 100 of the world's leading silicon photonics manufacturers use FormFactor systems meaning every CPO chip that ships from any fab in that diagram gets tested on FormFactor equipment.

6. Teradyne (TER): Teradyne sits in the E/O Testing layer alongside Keysight and FormFactor. As silicon photonics chips get more complex, integrating lasers, modulators, photodetectors and electronic drivers on a single chip,  the test complexity explodes. Teradyne's automated test equipment platforms are expanding from traditional semiconductor testing into photonic integrated circuit validation. It also has a robotics division, which ties the silicon photonics thesis directly back to the humanoid supply chain story.

7. EXFO (EXFO): EXFO is a fiber optic and network testing specialist that has been building testing platforms specifically for coherent optical and silicon photonics applications. It sits directly in the E/O Testing box of the supply chain map. It's a smaller cap, which means the upside from the optical buildout is amplified relative to its size. It is one of the few companies that tests live network optical performance meaning it gets pulled in not just at the component manufacturing stage but every time a data center expands or upgrades.

8. Foxconn Industrial Internet (FXCOF): Foxconn appears twice in that supply chain diagram in both Photonics Assembly and Optical Interconnect and that is before you even consider its server business. Q1 2026 revenue hit $66.6 billion, up 29.7% year over year, with AI servers now representing more than 50% of total server revenue. Its silicon photonics CPO switches entered mass production in Q3 2026 with full year shipments forecast at 10,000 units. It is boosting capex 30% specifically for AI infrastructure. Almost no retail investors think of Foxconn as a silicon photonics play and that's the opportunity.

9. Sumitomo Electric (SMTOY): Sumitomo appears in two places on that supply chain diagram as an optical interconnect component supplier and in the laser/photodiode layer. The company has been a foundational supplier to the fiber optic industry for decades and is now scaling its silicon photonics packaging and optical connectivity capabilities for AI data center applications.

10. Synopsys (SNPS): Every silicon photonics chip has to be designed before it can be manufactured. Synopsys is the dominant EDA software company for photonic integrated circuit design and sits at the very top left of that supply chain map. The shift toward co-packaged optics means optical and electronic chip design have to be co-simulated, exactly the kind of complex, multi physics workflow Synopsys is built for. Its photonics design tools are already embedded across every major chip company in that diagram. The more complex the chip, the more customers pay for Synopsys tools.

Milk Road Pro is tracking every layer of the silicon photonics supply chain, from Broadcom and Marvell to the testing names most investors still don’t know.

Join Milk Road Pro for the full breakdown and for all our AI trades for just $1 using  the link below!

text:
$SIVE is up ~1,700% in 2026, and following every tracked call on it returned +234% YTD on schwerelos.

Sivers makes the laser arrays for co-packaged optics, the interconnect AI data centers need to move data at 1.6 terabits. GlobalFoundries just signed them into its silicon-photonics platform, aimed at the $25B pluggable-optics market.

3 scored calls tracked, 1 closed in profit.

text:
💰 Optical Chip Startup Ayar Labs Secures $500 Million at $3.75 Billion Valuation

1. Ayar Labs has successfully raised $500 million in a new funding round that values the semiconductor startup at $3.75 billion.
2. The company specializes in optical computing technology that uses light rather than electricity to move data between artificial intelligence chips.
3. Investors are increasingly backing alternative hardware architectures to diversify the supply chain and overcome the bandwidth bottlenecks in traditional silicon.

Bobbi thinks:
With data center energy demands skyrocketing, the shift toward silicon photonics is becoming an essential evolutionary step for scaling next-generation supercomputers.

Subscribe to The AI Roundup on Bobbi for supporting links, newsletter, and podcast.

text:
🔵 Blue light breakthrough in perovskite LEDs!

Scientists have achieved a major advance in blue perovskite LEDs using a clever molecular trick: isomeric multi-hydrogen-bonding. By engineering molecules that form multiple hydrogen bonds in specific geometric arrangements, researchers dramatically improved the stability and efficiency of blue-emitting perovskite devices — historically the weakest link in full-color perovskite display technology. This could be a game-changer for next-gen displays and solid-state lighting, potentially rivaling or surpassing OLED technology in both performance and manufacturing cost. Blue perovskite LEDs have long lagged behind their red and green counterparts — this breakthrough may finally close the gap and unlock commercial viability for the entire perovskite LED ecosystem.

#PerovskiteLED #DisplayTechnology #MaterialsScience #Photonics #Nanotechnology

Companies in this space: Samsung Electronics ($SSNLF, OTC), Universal Display Corporation ($OLED, NASDAQ), Merck KGaA ($MKKGY, OTC)

text:
If you wanted to build a 7-figure retirement portfolio, would you only buy the AI names everyone already loves?

Or would you study the supply chain before the crowd catches on?

My AI optical infrastructure watchlist:

$GLW 
$AXTI 
$IQE.L
$AIXA  
$AMS  
The theme is simple:
AI data centers are moving more data than ever.
That means fiber, lasers, photonics, substrates and optical interconnects matter more than ever.
Which one is the most underrated?

Recommended if you track CPO / photonics

text:
$POET is not just a small-cap semiconductor stock.

The market often overlooks POET Technologies as a niche name, but its exposure is highly asymmetric in the AI infrastructure buildout.

Its real exposure is:

1.Silicon photonics-based optical interconnects
2.Ultra-high-speed AI data center connectivity
3.Power-efficient chip-to-chip communication
4.Next-generation optical engine architecture
5.Scaling bottlenecks in AI compute infrastructure

The key question now:

Is $POET still an early-stage AI infrastructure winner, or already overextended after the repricing?

From my perspective, this is a high-risk, high-reward structural option on AI data movement — not a traditional semiconductor valuation story.

Key driver here is simple:

AI compute scaling is increasingly limited by data transfer, not just processing power.

If optical interconnect adoption accelerates, $POET sits in a potentially critical bottleneck solution layer.

But this is not a stable compounder.

It is a speculative infrastructure bet with execution and adoption risk still unresolved.

Position sizing and timing matter significantly here.

Do not treat it like a blue-chip AI name.

Treat it like an asymmetric infrastructure option on the future of data movement.
